items.source: The Dark Side Of The Boom / Weekender
items.title: Warsh drops 131-word FOMC statement — Greenspan-era brevity signals full regime change
items.idea: Kevin Warsh's first FOMC statement clocked in at 131 words — the lowest since Greenspan's January 2002 (128 words). Powell's final April statement was 341 words. Statement stripped all forward guidance. Nine of 18 participants now project at least one rate hike this year. One policymaker declined to submit dots. Rates held at 3.50–3.75% on unanimous 12-0 vote. 2Y UST spiked 14bps to 4.19%, highest since February.
items.angle: The dot plot used to be the product; Warsh is making the press conference the black box. Without forward guidance, every FOMC meeting is now a vol event. Price that into options premium.
items.tweetA: Warsh's first FOMC statement: 131 words. Greenspan averaged 128. Not since 2002 has the Fed communicated this little on purpose. Without forward guidance, you can't model the reaction function. Every meeting is now an event risk. Vol is being mispriced at every tenor.
items.tweetB: 9 of 18 Fed participants now project rate hikes. The dot plot used to be a road map. Under Warsh, it's becoming a suggestion box — he refused to submit his own dots. The market is still pricing a friendly Fed. The Fed just told you that's not what they are.

charts.desc: FOMC statement word count chart (Greenspan to Warsh); 2Y yield spike chart

items.title: 2s10s: 38bps → 24bps post-FOMC, next target 15.9bps — the curve refuses to behave
items.idea: Post-FOMC bear-flattening brought 2s10s from 38.2bps to 24.4bps in two days. Sell-side target: 15.9bps (2025 low). 2Y at 4.19%, 10Y at 4.46%, 30Y at 4.91%. $24B 5Y TIPS reopening stopped through at 1.955% with lowest dealer allocation in over a year. Market pricing 45bps of hikes over next year; ~40% odds of 2026 hike.
items.angle: TIPS auction clearing at 1.955% real yield while nominal 10Y sits at 4.46% means inflation breakevens are collapsing on the Iran peace deal. The flattener IS the trade. Scale in on intraday concession.
items.tweetA: 2s10s: 38bps to 24bps in two days. Target: 15.9bps — the 2025 low. A $24B TIPS auction stopped through at 1.955% real yield with near-zero dealer allocation. The bid is real. Curve flatteners are the trade of the week.
items.tweetB: The bond market is simultaneously pricing Fed hikes (2Y at 4.19%) AND falling inflation (10Y rallied on Iran deal). That's incoherent. Either the 2Y is wrong or the 10Y is wrong. One of them has to move. I know which way I'm leaning. $TLT $SHY

charts.desc: 🔥 2s10s bearish flattening chart with weekly momentum, TLINE support and next targets annotated

items.title: 'Stayflation' — not stagflation, just inflation that refuses to leave while real incomes fall
items.idea: New framework: 'stayflation' — inflation remaining elevated for reasons unrelated to the labor market, while real wages and real incomes are negative. Core PCE inflation expected at 3.45% YoY for May; headline PCE near 4.1%. Real disposable income excluding transfer payments has slipped into negative territory YoY. Saving rate at 2.6%, lowest since mid-2022. Households increasingly spending via lower savings and more borrowing.
items.angle: Stayflation is more dangerous for rate-cut narratives than stagflation — because it doesn't imply a recession that would force the Fed's hand. The Fed can hold indefinitely in this regime.
items.tweetA: New framework: 'stayflation.' Not stagflation — no collapsing employment. Just inflation that refuses to leave while real incomes go negative. Saving rate at 2.6%. Households spending via borrowing. The Fed can't cut because inflation won't cooperate. They don't have to cut because no recession is forcing them.
items.tweetB: Core PCE expected at 3.45% for May. Headline close to 4.1%. Fed's target is 2%. If you thought the easing cycle would resume by H2 2026 — you need a new thesis. 'Indefinite hold' is now the consensus at every major bank. Markets are still priced for eventual cuts. That's the gap.

items.title: Real retail sales: no growth in 4 years — K-shaped consumer with saving rate at cycle lows
items.idea: May nominal retail sales +6.9% YoY but real inflation-adjusted sales still BELOW April 2022 peak — no growth in four years. World Cup and Knicks playoff spending likely exaggerated strength. Real disposable income negative YoY. Saving rate 2.6% (lowest since mid-2022). High-income households (wealth-effect driven) spending vigorously; middle/lower income treading water. Tax refund tailwind fading.
items.angle: The headline retail sales number is a nominal fiction. Households are liquidating savings to maintain spending. When savings hit zero, consumption growth stops. We may be closer to that inflection than the market thinks.
items.tweetA: May retail sales looked strong. Strip out inflation: real retail sales are still BELOW April 2022. No real growth in four years. Saving rate 2.6%, lowest since mid-2022. Consumers aren't spending more — they're spending the same nominal dollars that buy less.
items.tweetB: The K-shaped consumer: high income — wealth-effect fueled, spending vigorously. Middle/lower — real disposable income negative YoY, savings nearly depleted, borrowing more. These are two completely different economies. The headline retail number averages them into something that misleads.

charts.desc: Real vs nominal retail sales since 2020, 4-year flatline in real terms

items.source: The Bond Beat / Dark Side of the Boom
items.title: 10Y at 4.46% vs 4.25% CPI — bond market still pricing return to 2% inflation; Warsh could force a reprice
items.idea: 10-year Treasury at 4.46% vs CPI at 4.25% = barely positive real yield. Bond market implicitly betting the Fed gets back to 2%. If Fed raises inflation target to 3%, the 10Y may need to reprice to ~5.5%. Historical context: every Fed Chair since Volcker left with LOWER 10Y yields than they started — except Powell. Warsh starting at elevated yields with inflation 200bps above target.
items.angle: The 10Y is a bet on the Fed winning the inflation fight at 4.46%. If Warsh's task force concludes 2% is too aggressive, the repricing of the long end would be violent.
items.tweetA: 10Y Treasury at 4.46%. CPI at 4.25%. Real yield: barely 21bps. That's not a hawkish bond market — that's one still betting the Fed beats inflation back to 2%. If that bet breaks — if the inflation target moves or expectations de-anchor — the repricing to 5.5%+ could be violent. $TLT
items.tweetB: Every Fed Chair since Volcker ended their term with LOWER yields than they started. Except Powell. Warsh is inheriting the exception. Starting elevated, with inflation still 200bps above target, and a market that hasn't fully priced rate hikes back in. That's the setup.

charts.desc: 🔥 10Y yield by Fed Chair overlaid with each tenure; S&P 500 performance one year before/after new Fed chairs

items.source: The Dark Side Of The Boom / Weekender
items.title: Warsh's 'credibility over comfort' doctrine — the Fed stops cushioning every equity wobble
items.idea: Warsh framework: credibility over comfort, inflation discipline over asset-price reassurance, clearer hierarchy between Fed and market. Implications: (1) No implicit put under AI capex multiples. (2) 'Markets can live with a restrictive Fed if the framework is understood.' But framework is opaque — 5 new task forces, no forward guidance. Key Warsh quotes: 'policy is uneven not simply restrictive,' 'inflation is a choice,' and complete opposition to forward guidance.
items.angle: The Fed put has been the implicit subsidy for every growth stock multiple expansion since 2009. Warsh just told you he doesn't believe in the put. This is a regime change, not a hawkish meeting.
items.tweetA: 'Inflation is a choice.' Warsh said it at his first press conference. If Powell operated as an implicit backstop for risk assets — Warsh is explicitly withdrawing that backstop. AI stocks trade on long-duration cash flows discounted at the risk-free rate. Do the math on what no put means.
items.tweetB: Warsh has 5 task forces reviewing the Fed's: communications, balance sheet, data sources, productivity framework, and inflation target. He refused to submit his own dots. He won't use forward guidance. This isn't a hawkish Fed — it's an unreadable one. Unreadable = higher vol premium at every meeting.

items.title: Crude -$7 on Iran MOU, prompt contango, but nuclear talks stall over Lebanon — deal fragility underpriced
items.idea: Crude fell ~$7/bbl on US-Iran MOU. Dubai crude fell into outright prompt contango for first time since February. Brent/WTI on edge of contango. BUT: Iran already delayed permanent peace negotiations after Lebanon fighting intensified. MOU is a memorandum, not a treaty. Hormuz reopening described as 'chaotic.' Commodity Context calls this the 'Memorandum of Versailles' — concessions that may breed resentment.
items.angle: The market bought the headline and sold the oil. But Hormuz reopening is chaotic and Iran already stalled on Lebanon. If the permanent deal fails, the $7 giveback reverses fast. Long crude optionality here is cheap.
items.tweetA: Crude falls $7 on the Iran MOU. Great. But: Iran has already delayed the PERMANENT deal talks because of Lebanon. The MOU is a ceasefire on paper. Hormuz reopening is 'chaotic.' The crude market priced a clean resolution. The geopolitical reality is anything but. $CL
items.tweetB: Dubai crude is in prompt contango for the first time since February. The market is pricing oil market surplus. But US gasoline crack spreads are near the 2022 seasonal ATH. Contango in crude + ATH product cracks = something doesn't add up in energy. $VLO $MPC

charts.desc: 🔥 Brent/WTI timespread chart showing plunge toward contango; speculative positioning

items.title: Speculative Brent shorts at near-record — largest weekly increase ever, 'rocket fuel' for crude rally
items.idea: Gross Brent short positions experienced steepest single-week climb on record — within ~1 million barrels of all-time high (December). Largest weekly reduction in net long position of 2026. US and ARA European inventories continuing to crater. Singapore modest build off extraordinarily low base. This positioning creates asymmetry: any peace deal setback or demand surprise triggers massive short-covering rally.
items.angle: The crowded short is the tell. Everyone is positioned for the peace dividend. The contrarian trade is long crude with a stop below current price. If Hormuz stalls or Lebanon escalates, you don't need a new thesis — just cover.
items.tweetA: Speculative Brent shorts just hit near the all-time record. Everyone is positioned for the Iran peace dividend in oil. Meanwhile US and European crude inventories are still draining. This is not a structural surplus setup — it's a sentiment-driven short that will hurt badly on any setback.
items.tweetB: The single biggest weekly increase in Brent gross short positions on record happened THIS WEEK. The short is consensus. US/ARA inventories still draining. Peace deal is fragile. I'm not saying buy crude — I'm saying the risk/reward of staying short has never been worse. $USO $BNO

charts.desc: Speculative positioning chart showing gross Brent shorts at near-record levels

items.source: Commodity Context / Dark Side of the Boom
items.title: US gasoline crack spreads near 2022 seasonal ATH — crude down, product margins surging
items.idea: Despite crude falling on the Iran deal, US gasoline crack spreads ripped higher and are on edge of breaking above 2022's all-time seasonal refining margin. Divergence between falling crude and rising product cracks suggests refinery capacity constraints independent of geopolitical supply. Revised WTI framework: $79 Q3, $75 Q4, ~$80 full-year 2026, ~$75 for 2027. Downside risk flagged. Tail risk asymmetric.
items.angle: Crude falls and gasoline spreads stay high = refiners win. VLO, MPC, PSX in a sweet spot. The margin compression risk from crude rallying is diminished if the crack holds.
items.tweetA: Crude at $7 lower on the Iran deal. US gasoline crack spreads... near 2022 seasonal all-time highs. The geopolitical crude discount is flowing to refiner margins, not to consumers at the pump. $VLO $MPC $PSX are in a sweet spot nobody is talking about.
items.tweetB: Lower oil is supposed to be a gift for consumers. It is — until the crack spreads show refined products haven't followed crude lower. Gasoline cracks near ATH = refiners capturing the spread. Consumer relief will be partial and lagged. PCE Thursday will confirm. $XOP vs $VLO spread trade.

items.source: Dark Side Of The Boom / Bond Beat
items.title: Iran nuclear talks stall over Lebanon before they begin — MOU vs. permanent peace is a wide gap
items.idea: Bloomberg (cited by Bond Beat): 'Iran delayed the start of negotiations over a permanent peace deal with the US after fighting intensified in southern Lebanon.' The MOU signed this week was 14 points — a ceasefire framework, not a treaty. Permanent settlement would need to resolve Iran's nuclear programme, regional security concerns, and political interests of all parties. Commodity Context calls this the 'Memorandum of Versailles' — US concessions that may breed resentment.
items.angle: Markets priced the MOU as a peace treaty. It isn't. This is the most important risk factor for every trade that benefited from the Iran deal: risk-on, lower crude, tighter spreads. Lebanon is the tripwire.
items.tweetA: Iran has already delayed PERMANENT peace talks before they even started — Lebanon flared up. The market priced an MOU as if it were a peace treaty. Iran still has nukes to negotiate, regional proxies to manage, and Lebanon on fire. The crude and risk-on trades that priced a clean deal are now asymmetrically exposed.
items.tweetB: The 'Memorandum of Versailles' framing is apt. US concessions on paper. Iran stalls on Lebanon immediately. Crude already $7 lower. Hormuz reopening 'chaotic.' The bond and equity markets both loved the headline. Neither is pricing the tail risk of this deal breaking down.

items.title: Fear & Greed in 'Fear' with S&P near ATH — breadth at 52% neutral, Momentum factor dominated this week
items.idea: Despite S&P near all-time highs following US-Iran MOU, Fear-Greed Index remains in 'Fear' territory. Only 52-56% of stocks above 50-day MA — neutral, not overbought. Best performing factor this week: Momentum (#1), Growth (#2). Worst: Low-Volatility, Private Equity. Full reversal from prior week (Value/Low-Beta dominated). Top YTD Plutus baskets: Industrial/Auto Analog Recovery, Rebuilding US Industrial Sovereignty, Google Ecosystem, AI Full-Stack Infrastructure, Agentic Economy.
items.angle: Sentiment is fear at ATH prices. That's historically bullish for continuation — not distribution. But Momentum outperforming at near-ATH prices also means violent mean reversion risk when it stops.
items.tweetA: S&P near all-time highs. Fear & Greed: 'Fear.' Only 53% of stocks above 50-day MA. This is not euphoria — it's a wall of worry. Historically, markets climbing a wall of worry with neutral breadth tend to keep climbing. Sentiment alone isn't a top signal.
items.tweetB: Factor rotation this week: Momentum #1, Growth #2, Low-Vol and Private Equity worst. That's a 180 from the prior week (Value/Low-Beta dominated). Two weeks of violent factor rotation = nobody knows what to own. In that environment, concentration in crowded names is a risk, not a strategy.

charts.desc: Fear-Greed historical overlay with S&P; breadth indicator (% stocks above 50DMA)

items.source: Dark Side Of The Boom (Goldman / Pasquariello)
items.title: Goldman: HF gross + net exposure at 97th percentile — crowded and vulnerable to sharp reversals
items.idea: Goldman Sachs Global Head of HF Coverage Tony Pasquariello: gross and net hedge fund exposure near 97th percentile historically. Bull trend intact but dispersion widening beneath headline. AI capex — market rewards those receiving the cheques (picks-and-shovels) while scrutinizing those writing them (hyperscalers). Lower oil = inflation cushion that makes Fed the primary constraint on next leg higher.
items.angle: 97th percentile exposure reading tells you the magnitude of potential reversal when triggers appear — not the timing. High exposure + tight stops + any macro shock = waterfall.
items.tweetA: Goldman's head of HF coverage: gross and net HF exposure at the 97th percentile. Not a direction call — the 97th can stay elevated for months. But it's a severity signal. When this unwinds, it won't unwind politely. The bigger the crowding, the sharper the reversal.
items.tweetB: Pasquariello at Goldman: 'the market rewards those receiving the AI capex cheques while scrutinizing those writing them.' Most important AI trade framing right now. NVDA/ARM/Marvell are receiving. MSFT/META/AMZN/GOOG are writing. Check your portfolio side of that ledger.

items.title: Since January, S&P gains entirely from AI + energy — everything else is negative YTD
items.idea: Chart cited in Bond Beat shows all 2026 S&P 500 gains concentrated in two sectors: AI-related names and energy. Every other sector is negative YTD. Tech is +21% vs S&P +9.6%. Extreme concentration — not a broad bull market. This is why breadth at 52-56% doesn't match the index at ATH.
items.angle: A market up 9.6% held by 2 sectors is a dumbbell, not a bull market. When either stumbles, the index exposure of most portfolios gets a nasty surprise.
items.tweetA: The S&P 500 is up 9.6% YTD. Strip out AI and energy: everything else is negative on the year. This isn't a bull market. It's a two-sector phenomenon with an index label. When you buy SPY you're buying a concentrated position in AI + crude. Most people haven't processed that.
items.tweetB: AI is +21% YTD. Energy contributed before the Iran deal. Everything else: negative. The Fed is fighting inflation partly caused by those exact two sectors. Lower oil removes the energy contribution. The entire burden falls on AI. What does the AI trade look like when capex scrutiny picks up? $QQQ $SPY

charts.desc: 🔥 S&P 500 sector returns since Jan 2026 — only AI and energy positive, all others negative

items.title: EEM +30% YTD breaks to new ATH — EM outperforming US by 2000bps, 'multi-year run' forming
items.idea: EEM at $70.79, +30.06% YTD, outperforming S&P (9.36%) by over 2,000bps and breaking to new all-time highs on Friday. Key support on pullback: 50-day SMA at $65.37. BTC at -28.8% YTD, rejected twice at 200-day MA, at 200-week MA. SOXX +112.57% at fresh ATH after 100-point bounce in 9 sessions. Gold $4,120.85 lost 200-day MA for first time since bull run began in late 2023.
items.angle: EEM at +30% outperforming SPY by 20 percentage points is a structural signal. Dollar weakness, commodity exposure, lower oil = EM tailwinds. The chart is telling you something the narratives haven't caught up to.
items.tweetA: EEM broke to new ALL-TIME HIGHS this week. +30% YTD. Outperforming the S&P by 2000bps. This is not a trade — it's a regime shift. Weaker dollar, commodity tailwinds, EM domestic demand recovery. We may be in the early innings of an EM decade. $EEM
items.tweetB: World according to this week's closes: SOXX +112% YTD at ATH. EEM +30% at ATH. S&P +9.4%. Gold -2.6%, just lost 200-day MA for first time since 2023. BTC -28.8%. Three completely different macro views embedded in one week's closes. Which is right?

charts.desc: 🔥 EEM breakout to new ATH; SOXX bounce; Gold 200-day MA breakdown; BTC at 200-week MA

items.title: Intel +10% on Apple returning to Intel foundry — TSMC capacity constraints opening the door
items.idea: INTC surged 10% to new ATH on Apple going to Intel for chip manufacturing. Historical context: Intel refused to make iPhones in the smartphone era — handed TSMC the keys to the mobile chip kingdom. TSMC capacity constraints are now creating the opening. Intel also hired Seok-Hee Lee (ex-SK Hynix veteran, former Intel) to lead advanced packaging and back-end engineering. Intel Foundry needs anchor customer volume; Apple is the perfect anchor.
items.angle: Apple returning to Intel is not just revenue — it's a credibility event for Intel Foundry. Other companies waiting to see if Intel can execute will now watch the Apple relationship. Execution is the variable.
items.tweetA: Intel missed the mobile era because they told Apple the market wasn't big enough. TSMC got the business, became what it is, and now TSMC has capacity constraints. Apple is going back to Intel. 'Opportunity knocking twice.' $INTC up 10% to new ATH. Execution is everything from here.
items.tweetB: Apple going to Intel Foundry solves Intel's core problem: it needs anchor customers with volume to justify the capex to get advanced nodes to cost parity. Apple is the best anchor customer in the world. If INTC can execute on packaging and yield with Apple watching — the entire Intel Foundry thesis reprices. $INTC

items.title: AMD acquires MEXT.ai — intelligent DRAM/NAND tiering targets $400B AI memory spend
items.idea: AMD acquiring MEXT.ai, software that uses AI to manage data tiering between fast DRAM and slower NAND flash. Memory estimated at 36-40% of AI infrastructure CapEx — at $1T total AI capex in 2027, that's ~$400B. MEXT: identifies hot/cold DRAM data, moves cold data to cheaper NAND, predicts what needs to come back before compute needs it. Removes need to add infinite DRAM. Bullish for NAND; signals new era of software-level memory optimization pushing back on DRAM pricing power.
items.angle: Bullish NAND (MU NAND, WDC, KIOXIA), potentially a headwind for pure HBM/DRAM at the margin. Every CFO asking 'do you really use all that DRAM?' is now finding answers via software tiering.
items.tweetA: AMD is acquiring MEXT.ai — software that predicts which DRAM data needs to stay hot vs. which can move to cheaper NAND flash. Why it matters: memory is ~$400B of the expected $1T AI CapEx in 2027. If software can replace $100B of DRAM with NAND — seismic shift in the memory stack. Bullish $MU NAND.
items.tweetB: Memory is 36-40% of AI CapEx. Market has been pricing this as pure DRAM/HBM demand. But DRAM prices keep rising until they don't — until the industry finds workarounds. MEXT is the first major software-tiering acquisition. This is the beginning of the software pushback on memory pricing power.

charts.desc: Memory capex share estimate chart (~36% of AI CapEx going to memory)

items.title: PicoJool 200G VCSELs at 38GHz — breaks the 100G ceiling for AI scale-up, GaAs vs InP implications
items.idea: Startup PicoJool announced 200G VCSEL lasers for AI scale-up networking at ~38 GHz bandwidth — far ahead of prior literature (~25-30 GHz SOTA from 2022). VCSELs use cheap GaAs vs. scarce InP used in coherent optics. If 200G VCSELs work, InP TAM for scale-up networking shrinks. Lumentum's 980nm/1060nm VCSEL roadmap (no aluminum oxide = higher reliability) is the hedge. Key caveat: unproven in production; reliability questions remain.
items.angle: Early-stage and unproven. But the strategic direction is clear: the industry WANTS a cheap GaAs alternative to InP. Next 12 months in photonics will be fascinating to watch. $LITE $COHR watch for AI networking optical share shift.
items.tweetA: PicoJool announced 200G VCSEL lasers at 38 GHz bandwidth this week. VCSELs have been stuck below 100G for years. This isn't shipping yet — but if it works, the TAM for scarce InP lasers in AI scale-up networking shrinks significantly. The photonics supply chain just got a new variable. $LITE $COHR
items.tweetB: Most AI optical networking uses InP lasers — scarce, expensive, export-restricted. VCSELs use cheap GaAs. PicoJool says they've cracked 200G in GaAs. Unproven in production, but the strategic direction is clear: the industry WANTS a cheap alternative to InP. The next 12 months in photonics will be fascinating.

charts.desc: Modulation bandwidth chart for 850nm VCSELs showing historical progress and PicoJool's 38 GHz claim; S21 parameter measurement

items.title: Wall Street chose Ethereum. The market chose Ethereum's apps. ETH itself is bleeding.
items.idea: Institutional Ethereum adoption is accelerating: Janus Henderson ($480B AUM) invested in ENA, Morpho raised record $175M at $2B valuation (largest DeFi raise in history), BlackRock's BUIDL holds $2B+ onchain, JPMorgan put first tokenized MMF on Ethereum, SharpLink holds 875,000 ETH. Paradox: value is accruing to application tokens (ENA, MORPHO), not to ETH itself. Platform winning, asset losing.
items.angle: TCP/IP problem applied to crypto: everyone uses the protocol, nobody gets paid for owning it. The bulls have the adoption story; the bears have the tokenomics. Both are right.
items.tweetA: BlackRock, JPMorgan, Janus Henderson, Franklin Templeton, VanEck, Apollo — all building on Ethereum. $2B in BUIDL. $175M record DeFi raise at Morpho. 875,000 ETH held by ex-BlackRock team. The institutional bid for Ethereum is unambiguous. The bid for ETH itself is not. Explain that.
items.tweetB: Morpho raised $175M at a $2B valuation — largest DeFi raise in history. Value went to MORPHO token holders and equity investors. Not to ETH. Janus Henderson invested in ENA, not ETH. Pattern: institutions love Ethereum the platform, buy the apps on top, ignore the base layer asset. That's the ETH thesis gap.

items.title: Ebola Bundibugyo: 1,000+ cases, NO proven vaccine, contact tracing failing in conflict zone
items.idea: DRC/Uganda Ebola outbreak topped 1,000 reported infections. Dominant strain: Bundibugyo — NO proven vaccine (all existing capacity built for different strains). CDC: $107M emergency funding. WHO: $500M containment plan. 4 vaccine candidates; 2 potentially ready for trials in ~2 months (August 2026 trigger). Contact tracing failing due to armed conflict and community mistrust in eastern DRC. Patients not staying in care facilities. Hoax rumors converting facilities from sinks to targets.
items.angle: Slow-moving tail risk markets are not pricing. If outbreak crosses into urban corridors or World Cup host countries via travel restrictions failure, downstream effects severe. Biotech with Ebola vaccine candidates to watch.
items.tweetA: 1,000+ Ebola cases in DRC/Uganda. The strain is Bundibugyo — no proven vaccine. Contact tracing failing because eastern DRC is a conflict zone. Patients not staying in facilities. Four vaccine candidates; earliest clinical trial: August 2026. This is a 2014-style trajectory so far. Watch it closely.
items.tweetB: The outbreak financing loop is broken: donors release money when case curves scare capitals, but field teams need money BEFORE the curve is visible. Underfunded surveillance → blind spots → sudden jumps → emergency tranches that arrive too late. CDC's $107M and WHO's $500M are following that playbook exactly.

items.title: US using Ebola travel rules as World Cup liability management — 21-day entry rule shifts risk to others
items.idea: US implemented 21-day entry rule for travelers from affected regions and established Kenya quarantine facility. Explicit framing: shifting Ebola exposure risk from American World Cup venues to transit states, European clubs with African rosters, and African delegations. DRC's leverage weak — immediate need is cash and vaccines; donors route funds through multilaterals while keeping control over procurement, data, and evacuation. State Department memo comparing US vs. Chinese Ebola response.
items.angle: The 21-day entry rule is both public health and reputational liability hedge. It's also potential diplomatic friction with DRC and Africa. Any African business executive subject to quarantine is effectively blocked from US travel. Soft power consequences.
items.tweetA: The US's Ebola 21-day entry rule isn't just public health — it's World Cup liability management. It shifts risk exposure from American venues to transit states, European clubs with African rosters, and African delegations. The geopolitics of disease control in 2026 are inseparable from sports diplomacy.
items.tweetB: DRC has 1,000 Ebola cases and no Bundibugyo vaccine. The US response: 21-day quarantine rules + a State Dept memo comparing its response favorably to China's. This is soft power competition dressed up as public health policy. Africa will notice which framing dominates.

items.source: The Dark Side Of The Boom / Weekender
items.title: Post-Hormuz behavioral shift: Asian energy diversification accelerates — structurally bearish long-term crude
items.idea: The Weekender argues the most important consequence of Hormuz closure may be behavioral changes that outlive the immediate conflict. Asian oil importers expected to accelerate energy supply diversification and renewable investment. Producers forced to shut in barrels reconsidering export routes, infrastructure, strategic capacity. OPEC+ members may revisit spare capacity assumptions. Extended closure represented an unprecedented stress test — those who failed won't want to repeat the experience.
items.angle: The Hormuz shock may have just pulled forward 3-5 years of Asian energy policy. Solar, LNG diversification, alternative shipping route investment accelerating. Structurally bearish for Middle East pricing power.
items.tweetA: The Hormuz closure may be over. The lesson won't be. Asian oil importers are now accelerating renewable investment and supply diversification. OPEC+ producers are rethinking spare capacity assumptions. The geopolitical oil shock just pulled forward years of energy transition policy. The long-term crude bear case just got stronger.
items.tweetB: If you're bullish long-term on Gulf oil producers, ask yourself: after months of Hormuz disruption, what Asian policy has NOT changed? LNG terminal contracts are being accelerated. Solar policy is being fast-tracked. Route diversification is happening. The stress test showed them what vulnerability looks like. They'll pay to avoid it.

items.title: Fed holds at 3.50-3.75%, ~40% 2026 hike odds, 'indefinite hold' is new consensus through end-2027
items.idea: Unanimous 12-0 hold. Half of 2026 dots now project hikes. Core PCE median revised to 3.3% for 2026. Multiple banks (ING, GS, JPM, Barclays) updated to 'indefinite hold' or 'hold through end-2027.' Rate cut probability repriced: 'markets aren't looking for rates to come down before 2028.' GS estimates ~40% probability of 2026 hike despite holding baseline. 'June Swoon' (equity/bond selloff together) is the macro risk scenario.
items.angle: Nobody talks about rate cuts anymore. An indefinite hold with 4.25% CPI means real Fed Funds rate is effectively flat — not tight. Tightening is happening via credibility, not rate levels. Different animal to navigate.
items.tweetA: 'Nobody talks about rate cuts anymore. That was so 2025.' Multiple sell-side banks changed Fed forecast to 'indefinite hold through end-2027.' Markets still priced for eventual easing. Consensus: cuts don't come until 2028. That's a 2-year duration mismatch. $TLT
items.tweetB: Fed Funds at 3.50-3.75%. CPI at 4.25%. Real Fed Funds rate: NEGATIVE. This isn't tight monetary policy — it's restrictive on paper, accommodative in reality. Warsh knows this. That's why 9 of 18 dots project hikes. The gap between rate level and inflation level is the story.

charts.desc: Dot plot evolution showing shift from cuts to hikes

items.title: University of Michigan: 54% of households expect unemployment to rise — matches GFC and 1990s recession depths
items.idea: U of M sentiment index shows net 54% of households expect unemployment to rise over next 12 months — matching GFC depths and early 1980s/1990s recession lows. Yet actual retail sales strong, payrolls decent, GDP tracking ~3% annualized. K-shaped explanation: high-income drives spending data, everyone else drives sentiment survey. ING: 'workers themselves aren't believing it.' Weak jobs turnover pointing to slowing wage growth. Real wages negative.
items.angle: Sentiment at GFC lows with spending still positive = the K-shaped divergence is so extreme the averages are useless. When the savings buffer is exhausted for the lower half, sentiment becomes predictive. We may be approaching that point.
items.tweetA: 54% of US households expect unemployment to rise in the next 12 months. That matches GFC lows and early 1980s/1990s recession depths. Meanwhile retail sales are strong and GDP is tracking 3%. Either the consumer knows something the data doesn't yet — or the K-shaped divergence is so extreme the averages are useless.
items.tweetB: Sentiment at GFC lows. Spending at cycle highs. Saving rate nearly depleted. Real wages negative YoY. This is the end game of a cycle where the upper income tier carries the headline and the median household silently deteriorates. The moment the top-tier wealth effect fades, those averages gap down fast.

items.title: SOXX +112% YTD at ATH after 100pt bounce in 9 sessions; AAOI showing distribution warning signs
items.idea: SOXX at $639.45, +112.57% YTD, fresh ATH. 100-point bounce in 9 sessions from recent lows after a 3x volume selloff. NVDA at $210.69, recovered 21-day EMA and 50-day SMA on Friday after selling off from $240 peak. Key NVDA levels: $225 upside, $189.68 (200-day SMA, below 14x forward P/E) as add point. AAOI (optical/photonics, +364% YTD): making lower highs AND lower lows, selling off on volume, lost 50-day SMA — potential distribution in the leading photonics name.
items.angle: SOXX at +112% at ATH is the headline. The canary: AAOI at +364% showing classic distribution — lower highs, lower lows, volume on down days. AAOI often leads the AI infrastructure trade at inflection points.
items.tweetA: SOXX at +112% YTD, fresh ATH. 100-point bounce in 9 sessions. Semiconductor cycle not dead. But look at AAOI: +364% YTD and now making lower highs and lower lows, lost 50-day SMA on volume. The leading photonics name is showing distribution. That's worth watching before adding more AI infra. $SOXX $AAOI
items.tweetB: NVDA at $210.69, back above the 50-day SMA. Forward P/E drops below 14x at $189.68 (200-day SMA). At 14x forward for the dominant AI chip platform — interesting risk/reward. But SOXX at +112% YTD means you're not buying distress, you're buying momentum at ATH. Different risk profile entirely. $NVDA $SOXX

charts.desc: 🔥 SOXX ATH chart with 100-point bounce annotated; NVDA key levels; AAOI lower highs/lower lows distribution pattern; SPX above 50DMA breadth chart

items.source: Dark Side Of The Boom (JPMorgan semis note) / Haymaker
items.title: JPMorgan: Semiconductor concentration makes sector more prone to VaR shock-driven liquidation cascades
items.idea: JPMorgan's note (summarized by Dark Side of the Boom) argues that as concentration in semiconductor names has grown, the sector is increasingly prone to 'VaR shocks' — periods where institutions breach risk limits and are forced to cut exposure rapidly, feeding the move and creating liquidation cascades. Mechanism: rising volatility means same position = much more risk, stops easier to hit, liquidity thins. This is the institutional version of the same concentration risk Goldman flagged (97th percentile HF exposure). Two separate sources, same warning. Haymaker's Friday POW featured a Macy's (Thanksgiving Day Parade retailer) investment-grade bond described as yielding like 'a New York steak' — paywalled for details.
items.angle: Two independent sources flagging semiconductor concentration risk this week is the signal. When JPMorgan's prime brokerage and Goldman's HF coverage both flag the same crowding — the setup is mature. $SOXX options vol may be underpriced for tail scenarios.
items.tweetA: Two separate notes this week flag the same semiconductor risk: (1) Goldman: HF exposure at 97th percentile. (2) JPMorgan: semi concentration means VaR shocks trigger forced selling cascades. Neither is a direction call. Both say: when the unwind comes, it comes fast and hard. $SOXX options structure matters.
items.tweetB: JPMorgan's framework: high semiconductor concentration = VaR-driven selling cascades when volatility spikes. When risk limits are breached, investors must cut — which feeds the move. SOXX at +112% YTD. When did you last stress-test that position at 2x normal vol? That's the exercise to run this weekend.
